sources_note: Growing guidance on this page draws on Australian state agriculture departments (Northern Territory, Queensland and Western Australia), the Australian mango industry, university horticulture guides, and the Rare Fruit Council of Australia archives.

core.intro: Across Australia the basics of growing mango are the same: full sun, free-draining soil, a frost-free spot, and a cool, dry spell before flowering so the tree sets fruit instead of leaf. The state sections cover the climate, regions, harvest timing and pests where you are; the notes below apply anywhere mango will grow.

sections.heading: Choosing a variety
sections.body: Kensington Pride (often shortened to KP) is the classic Australian mango and still most of the crop. R2E2, a Queensland selection from the variety Kent, gives large, low-fibre fruit; Honey Gold is a popular modern Australian variety; and late types such as Keitt and Brooks stretch the season into autumn. Thai types like Nam Doc Mai, Khiew Sawoey and Chok Anan, and Indian types like Alphonso, Kesar and Mallika, suit warm gardens and are widely stocked. Decide on flavour and season first, then pick a named variety to match; the in-stock table above shows what is available right now.

sections.heading: Seedling or grafted? The polyembryonic trick
sections.body: Mango is unusual: many varieties are polyembryonic, meaning a seed produces several clones of the mother tree, so the seedlings come true to type. Kensington Pride and most South-East Asian mangoes (such as Nam Doc Mai) are polyembryonic, which is why a Kensington Pride seedling can fruit much like its parent. Indian and Florida varieties (Alphonso, Haden, Irwin, Keitt, Kent) are monoembryonic: their seedlings are unpredictable hybrids and must be grafted. For a known fruit, buy a named grafted tree, or a true-to-type seedling of a polyembryonic variety.

sections.heading: Do you need two trees?
sections.body: No. Most mango varieties are self-fertile, so a single tree will set fruit on its own. The small flowers are pollinated mainly by flies and other insects rather than honeybees, so a good crop depends far more on warm, dry, still weather during flowering than on having a second tree nearby.

sections.heading: Planting and soil
sections.body: Mango wants full sun and deep, free-draining soil; a sandy loam is ideal. It will not tolerate waterlogging, so avoid spots where water pools and plant on a mound if your soil is heavy. Young trees are frost-tender, so give them a warm, sheltered position and protect them over their first winters where light frosts occur. Container-grown trees can go in through the warmer months as long as you water them in well (see our guide on when to plant).

sections.heading: Water and feeding
sections.body: Water young trees regularly to get them established (a guide is up to 100 litres a tree a week over the first couple of years) and mulch with coarse straw kept off the trunk; mature trees are more drought-hardy but still crop better with steady water. A cool spell is what actually triggers a mango to flower, but running the tree a little dry from late autumn until flowering is thought to sharpen and synchronise that flowering, so growers ease off water and heavy nitrogen through winter, then water steadily again from flowering through fruit development and stop a few weeks before harvest. Feed with a complete fertiliser (the Queensland mango kit uses an N:P:K of about 15:4:11, at only 30 to 60 grams per young tree every six to eight weeks in the first year, building up with age), and put it on soil that is moist after watering rather than bone dry. Time the bulk of the nitrogen for just after harvest when the tree is flushing, and go easy on nitrogen at flowering and fruit set, since too much pushes leaves at the expense of fruit. Potassium is the key fruiting nutrient and is best split into several small doses through fruit fill, while boron and zinc are the trace elements that most often run short in mango (low boron shows as small holes in the leaves), so a soil and leaf test is the best guide to what your tree needs.

sections.heading: Harvest and ripening
sections.body: Mangoes are picked mature but still firm and green, then ripened off the tree at room temperature (about 18 to 22 degrees). A grafted tree usually starts bearing in about three to five years, sometimes sooner in the tropics. Take care with the sap: when the stalk is snapped the first spurt is caustic, and it both burns dark marks into the fruit skin (sapburn, worst in Kensington Pride) and can irritate your skin, so pick with a clean snap and stand the fruit stalk-down to drain.

sections.heading: Pruning
sections.body: Prune after harvest to keep the tree small and open. Mango flowers from the tips of its branches, so a compact, well-lit canopy carries plenty of fruiting terminals and is far easier to net, spray and pick. A backyard tree can be held to two or three metres with yearly pruning.

sections.heading: Buying tips
sections.body: Buy a named grafted tree, or a true-to-type polyembryonic seedling such as Kensington Pride, rather than an unknown seedling that may never fruit to type. Dwarf and semi-dwarf selections (stocked as Irwin, Palmer, Bambaroo and King Thai dwarfs, and Little Gem) suit large pots and small gardens. Note that Calypso (the brand name for variety B74) and Honey Gold are trademarked, managed varieties grown under licence: Honey Gold trees do reach nurseries, but Calypso is sold as fruit and is rarely offered as a backyard tree, so the table above shows the named varieties you can actually buy now.

faqs.q: How big does a mango tree get, and can I keep it small?
faqs.a: A mango can grow into a large tree of 10 metres or more, but a yearly prune after harvest easily holds it to two or three metres, and dwarf varieties such as Little Gem stay smaller again, which suits a big pot or a small backyard.

faqs.q: How do I ripen mangoes at home?
faqs.a: Mangoes are picked firm and green and ripen on the bench over several days; a ripe one gives a little at the stem end and smells sweet. To hurry it along, sit the fruit in a paper bag with a banana, and do not refrigerate an unripe mango because cold stops it ripening properly.

faqs.q: How long until a mango tree fruits?
faqs.a: A grafted mango usually starts bearing in about three to five years, sometimes sooner in the tropics. Seedlings of monoembryonic varieties take longer and the fruit is unpredictable, which is why named, grafted stock is worth paying for.

faqs.q: Why does mango sap burn the fruit and my skin?
faqs.a: The first sap that spurts out when you snap the stalk is caustic. It causes dark sapburn marks on the skin (worst in Kensington Pride) and can irritate your own skin, so harvest with a quick snap, keep the sap off the fruit, and stand picked fruit stalk-down to drain.

WA.intro: Growing mango in Western Australia. WA grows excellent mango, but in the tropical and subtropical north around Kununurra and Carnarvon, not in the cool south-west, so in Perth it is a warm-microclimate gamble rather than a sure thing.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Mango needs a warm to hot growing season and crops best where rainfall and humidity are low at flowering, which the northern WA dry season supplies. Young trees are killed by temperatures below 0.5 degrees and older trees are damaged by frost, so the cool, wet-winter south-west (including Perth and the Gingin district) is marginal: trees grow but flower and fruit unreliably and can be cut back by frost.

sections.heading: Where mango grows in WA
sections.body: The Ord River around Kununurra, in the east Kimberley, is WA's main mango district, with Carnarvon on the Gascoyne coast the other commercial area. Plantings thin out southward through Broome to Gingin, north of Perth, which is the cool, frost-prone southern edge of where mango will grow in the west.

sections.heading: Harvest window in WA
sections.body: WA opens the national mango season. Kununurra fruit comes in from about September, ahead of the eastern states, followed in turn by Broome, Carnarvon and Gingin, so the WA crop can run from spring through into autumn.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in WA
sections.body: The dry northern climate keeps anthracnose, the main mango disease in the humid east, much more in check at flowering, though it still needs watching. Mango seed weevil is not established in WA and is a declared pest that must be reported, which is one reason live mango plants face strict entry rules. Mediterranean fruit fly is widespread in WA and will attack ripening fruit.

sections.heading: Buying and shipping to WA
sections.body: WA quarantine means live plants from other states must be permitted on the Western Australian Organism List, and many eastern nurseries will not post mango plants here, partly because mango seed weevil is a declared pest WA is keeping out. So the in-stock table above is filtered to sellers that can ship to WA, which in practice is mostly WA nurseries plus the few interstate sellers that meet the rules.

faqs.q: Can you grow a mango tree in Perth?
faqs.a: You can grow the tree in a warm, sheltered, frost-free spot, but Perth's cool, wet winters make flowering and fruiting unreliable, and young trees are killed by temperatures below 0.5 degrees. WA's dependable mango comes from the tropical north around Kununurra and Carnarvon, not the south-west.

faqs.q: Which mango varieties suit WA's north?
faqs.a: In the Ord and at Carnarvon, Kensington Pride is the mainstay, with R2E2, Honey Gold and the late Keitt also grown. In a frost-free Perth microclimate, a hardy grafted Kensington Pride or a dwarf in a pot is the safest thing to try.

QLD.intro: Growing mango in Queensland. Queensland is mango country: it grows about a third of the national crop across the tropical north and subtropical south-east, and almost any Queensland garden with sun and drainage can grow one.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Queensland's warm to hot summers suit mango, and the key is the flowering trigger: cool winter weather from about June to August stimulates the tree to flower, while warm, humid or wet conditions at flowering cause poor fruit set and feed disease. The dry tropical north and the drier subtropical inland flower more reliably than the humid coast and wet tropics. Queensland grows about a third of Australia's mango; the Northern Territory grows the most.

sections.heading: Where mango grows in QLD
sections.body: The biggest districts are Mareeba and Dimbulah on the Atherton Tableland in the far north (about 55 per cent of Queensland production), and Bowen and the Burdekin in the dry tropics (about a third), with central and southern Queensland making up the rest. The town of Bowen gave Kensington Pride its other common name.

sections.heading: Harvest window in QLD
sections.body: Queensland harvest starts in the dry-tropical north around Bowen and the Burdekin in about mid-November, then Dimbulah and Mareeba through December, and moves into central and southern Queensland over late December and January. The southern districts finish around April.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in QLD
sections.body: Humidity is the main challenge. Anthracnose, a fungus, is the most serious mango disease and is worst when it rains during flowering and fruit set. Bacterial black spot also spreads in warm, humid, windy rain, and Kensington Pride is susceptible to it. Powdery mildew attacks flowers and young fruit, and Queensland fruit fly stings ripening fruit.

faqs.q: Which mango varieties grow best in Queensland?
faqs.a: Kensington Pride is the Queensland favourite and the bulk of the crop, with R2E2 and Honey Gold widely grown. In the humid tropics and along the coast, choose disease-tolerant types and keep the canopy open and airy to cut anthracnose.

faqs.q: Why is my Queensland mango flowering but not setting fruit?
faqs.a: Mango flowers are triggered by cool winter weather, but warm, wet or humid conditions during flowering cause poor fruit set and feed diseases like anthracnose. A sunny, airy position and a drier flowering help; humid coastal and tropical gardens are the hardest.

NSW.intro: Growing mango in New South Wales. NSW is the southern edge of Australia's mango range: the warm far-north coast grows it, and further south it becomes a sheltered-garden gamble.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Mango is a tropical and subtropical tree, and in Australia it is grown only as far south as northern New South Wales. The warm, frost-free far-north coast can ripen mango, but the humid coastal air raises disease pressure at flowering, and inland or more southern districts are too cool and frost-prone for a reliable crop.

sections.heading: Where mango grows in NSW
sections.body: The Northern Rivers, the warm coastal country around the Tweed, Brunswick and Richmond valleys, is the NSW mango area. South of there, mango is a backyard tree for warm, sheltered, frost-free spots rather than a dependable crop.

sections.heading: Harvest window in NSW
sections.body: NSW fruit is the last to ripen in the country, coming in from about January as the Queensland season moves south, and running on into autumn in the warmest coastal spots.

sections.heading: Pests and diseases in NSW
sections.body: Coastal humidity makes anthracnose (worst when it rains at flowering and fruit set) and bacterial black spot the main problems, and Queensland fruit fly will sting ripening fruit. Good air movement, an open canopy and prompt clean-up of fallen fruit all help.

faqs.q: Which mango varieties suit northern NSW?
faqs.a: On the warm far-north coast, stick to Kensington Pride and other early to mid-season varieties, choose disease-tolerant types, and give them the sunniest, most open, frost-free spot you can, since coastal humidity is the main enemy at flowering.

faqs.q: Can you grow mango south of the far-north coast in NSW?
faqs.a: It gets marginal fast. South of the Tweed and inland, mango is a warm-microclimate gamble: try a hardy grafted tree or a dwarf in a pot against a hot, north-facing wall, protect it from frost, and treat any fruit as a bonus.

VIC.intro: Growing mango in Victoria. Mango is not a Victorian crop: the state is too cool and too frosty for it to flower and fruit outdoors, so here it is a container or greenhouse curiosity rather than a backyard fruit tree.

sections.heading: Climate fit
sections.body: Mango is tropical to subtropical and does not tolerate frost; young trees are killed by temperatures below 0.5 degrees. Victoria's cool summers and cold, frosty winters mean a tree will not get the heat it needs to flower and ripen, and Australia's mango range stops well to the north, in northern New South Wales.

sections.heading: Growing mango in Victoria anyway
sections.body: If you want to try, treat mango as a potted plant: a dwarf grafted variety in a large pot, kept in the hottest, most sheltered corner of a Melbourne courtyard, or in a greenhouse or conservatory, and moved under cover for winter. Expect leaf growth far more often than flowers, and accept that fruit will be rare. For a home-grown crop, hardier subtropical fruit is a surer bet, and mango is best bought as fruit from the northern harvest.

faqs.q: Is it worth planting a mango outdoors in Victoria?
faqs.a: Not as an outdoor fruit tree. Victoria is too cool and too frosty (young mangoes are killed below about 0.5 degrees), and Australia's mango range stops in northern New South Wales. A dwarf tree in a large pot, moved into a greenhouse or sheltered courtyard for winter, may survive but will rarely fruit.

faqs.q: Why won't a mango fruit in Melbourne?
faqs.a: Mango needs a long, hot season and a frost-free winter to flower and ripen, and Melbourne provides neither. The tree may grow in a pot or greenhouse, but the climate will not let it set and ripen a crop reliably.
